Land Rover Discovery TD6/SDV6 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Discovery TD6/SDV6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 3.0 V6 306DT engine typically costs £6,500–£9,500 supply only, with fitting adding £1,600–£2,200. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Discovery.

mainEntity.name: Is the Discovery TD6 (306DT V6) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's a capable and powerful engine, but it has a known weakness — the crankshaft bearing design. With aggressive 5,000-mile oil changes and careful monitoring, it can be reliable. Without that maintenance, it is one of the more failure-prone modern JLR diesels.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Discovery TD6/SDV6 engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crankshaft bearing fatigue (catastrophic failure risk), turbocharger contamination from oil system issues, and diesel injector clogging. The crank bearing is the defining issue for this engine.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery TD6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. A D5 Discovery is a £15,000–£35,000 vehicle — an £8,000 recon is 23–53% of the car's value. This sits at or just below our 60% Land Rover scrap threshold, so evaluate condition carefully. A well-maintained example justifies replacement; a tired, high-mileage one may not.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Discovery TD6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 3.0 V6 diesel engine code is 306DT — used in both TD6 and SDV6 variants. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the crankshaft bearing failure on a 306DT?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — early intervention is key. The single most effective prevention is 5,000-mile oil changes with JLR-spec 5W30 or 0W30 oil. Some specialists also recommend oil analysis from 80,000 miles to monitor bearing wear. At the first sign of cold-start knock, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Crankshaft Bearing Failure (Catastrophic)
problems.symptoms: Cold-start knock or rumble, metallic tapping that fades as the engine warms up, oil pressure warning light, sudden engine seizure.
problems.repairCost: N/A — cannot be repaired; bottom-end rebuild or replacement required

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear cold knock, stop driving and book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: This is the single most important issue on the 306DT — the crankshaft bearing design has a fatigue life. The key defence is aggressive oil change intervals: 5,000 miles max, not the factory 15,000-mile schedule. If you hear a rumble or knock on cold start that fades as the engine warms up, have the oil pressure checked and the bearings inspected immediately. A bottom-end rebuild (£5,500–£7,000) is cheaper than a full engine replacement, but only if caught early.

problems.title: Oil Pump / Turbo Contamination
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, turbo whistle or whine, oil pressure warning, excessive exhaust smoke.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,000 (turbo rebuild or replacement) [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before turbo disintegrates)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if turbo noise or smoke appears

problems.recommendation: The 306DT's turbocharger is vulnerable to oil contamination from the crankcase ventilation system. Regular oil changes with the correct 5W30 or 0W30 JLR-spec oil are essential — and insist on OEM or OE-branded oil filters. If you notice a whistle, whine, or loss of power, have the turbo inspected without delay.

problems.title: Diesel Injector Failure
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, white or black smoke, loss of power, increased fuel consumption, engine rattle.
problems.recommendation: Ford-sourced injectors are common across the 306DT and can clog over time, especially if fuel filter changes are skipped. A periodic injector clean and using premium diesel can reduce the risk. If you notice rough running or smoke, have the injectors tested — replacing a full set is far cheaper than the collateral damage they can cause.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Discovery TD6/SDV6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Only if caught before the crank is scored — act on the first knock
Straightforward — rebuild or replace the turbo
Routine maintenance — do as a set
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Discovery TD6/SDV6? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented 5,000-mile oil change history (not 15,000-mile factory schedule)
* No cold-start knock or rumble — walk away if you hear anything unusual
* Full service history with JLR-spec oil and genuine filters
avoidIf: * Cold-start knock or rumble — this is the early warning sign of bearing failure
* No record of regular oil changes — 306DTs are unforgiving of neglect
* Engine oil pressure warning light — could indicate pump or bearing issues
* Turbo whistle or whine without service history — contamination may have occurred

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 306DT is a capable, torque-rich engine that powers the D5 Discovery well. But it has a known Achilles heel — the crankshaft bearing design. Buy only on documented evidence of 5,000-mile oil changes, and never ignore a cold-start knock. A well-maintained example is a reliable workhorse; a neglected one is a £7,000–£10,000 bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare Discovery TD6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Discovery TD6/SDV6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rumble or knock that fades as the engine warms — crank bearing warning sign
* Service History — including engine era (306DT V6) and confirmed 5,000-mile oil change intervals
* Oil Clarity — Check the dipstick; metallic glitter indicates bearing wear
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any whistle, whine, or lag?
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on the DPF and turbo
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
